coredata.dc:title: Role of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots on CuO nano-leaves as solar induced photo catalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Jamila, Ghulam Sughra
Sajjad, Shamaila
Leghari, Sajjad Ahmed Khan
Mahmood, Tariq
coredata.dc:description: A p-type transition metal oxide, copper oxide (CuO) was modified with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) to fabricate an effective CuO/NCQDs solar driven photo catalyst. The fabrication of single phase monoclinic CuO was confirmed by XRD. SEM images showed the leaf like morphology for CuO which appeared rough and densely packed in CuO/NCQDs composites. TEM images of CuO/NCQDs shows slightly distorted nano-leaves with NCQDs dispersed on them. HRTEM of CuO/NCQDs shows fringes with characteristic planes confirming that the addition of NCQDs has not distorted the crystal structure of CuO. SAED of CuO/NCQDs further confirms the crystalline nature of the as synthesized composite photo catalyst by exhibiting bright diffraction rings. These results further supported the XRD data. EDS spectra of NCQDs and CuO/NCQDs confirm the formation of pure nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and Cu, N, C and O atoms confirming that NCQDs are well incorporated on CuO Nano leaves. UV–Vis spectra showed a slight increase of band gap energies due to quantum confinement effects. PL spectra exhibited decreased photoluminescence intensity indicating suppression of recombination rate. The developed photocatalyst was applied for the degradation of harmful dye methyl orange. The composite catalyst showed superior degradation efficiency as compared to pure CuO nano-leaves attributed to enhanced visible light absorption and better charge separation ability due to introduction of NCQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuO nano-leaves
Nitrogen doped quantum dots
Quantum confinement effect
Solar light catalyst
Charge separation efficiency

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of pure NCQDs (A) FTIR of NCQDs (B) AFM of NCQDs (C).
XRD patterns of pure CuO and CuO/NCQDs composites with various concentrations of NCQDs; (a) CuO; NCQDs concentrations with (b) 0.5 mL; (c) 1.0 mL, and (d) 1.5 mL.
SEM images of pure CuO nano-leaves (A–B) and CuO/NCQDs (C–D) with NCQDs amount of 1.5 mL at different resolutions.
TEM and HRTEM images of CuO nano-leaves (A, B), CuO/NCQDs composites (D, E) and SAED patterns of CuO (C), CuO/NCQDs with 1.5 mL NCQDs (F).
EDX spectra of pure CuO (A), NCQDs (B) and CuO/NCQDs with NCQDs amount of 1.5 mL. (C)

UV–Vis absorption spectra of synthesized nanostructures (A) CuO (a), NCQDs (b), CuO/NCQDs composites with NCQDs amount 0.5 mL (c) and 1.5 mL (d). (B) Tauc plots of CuO (a), CuO/NCQDs of 0.5 mL (b), CuO/NCQDs of 1.5 mL (c) for in-direct bandgap energies and (C) Tauc plots for the corresponding direct bandgap energies.
Photoluminescence spectra of various samples; (a) CuO, (b) CuO/NCQDs with NCQDs amount of 0.5 mL and 1.5 mL (c).
FTIR spectra of CuO (a) CuO/NCQDs with varying amounts of NCQDs, 0.5 mL (b), 1.0 mL (c), 1.5 mL (d).
Raman spectrum of CuO/NCQDs nano photo catalyst with NCQDs amount of 1.5 mL.

Solar induced time dependent degradation profile of MO (A) without catalyst (a), with CuO (b), CuO/NCQDs with NCQDs amounts of 0.5 mL (c), 1.0 mL (d), 2.0 mL (e), 1.5 mL (f) and (B) kinetic study of MO degradation for CuO (a), CuO/NCQDs with varying amounts of NCQDs, 0.5 mL (b), 1.0 mL (c), 2.0 mL (d), 1.5 mL(e).
Recycling of the CuO/NCQDs photo catalyst over five cycles.
XRD and SEM of the CuO/NCQDs photo catalyst after photocatalytic experiments.
Synthesis recipe of CuO/NCQDs composites and experimental setup for photocatalytic experiments.
Proposed mechanism for the degradation of harmful organic dye (MO) through synthesized binary CuO/NCQDs photo catalyst.
Elemental composition of the synthesized samples.

head.ce:title: Role of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots on CuO nano-leaves as solar induced photo catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Islamic University, H-10, Islamabad, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Islamic University, H-10, Islamabad, Pakistan.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Islamabad, Pakistan.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Department, National Centre for Physics, Quaid-e-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Centre for Physics, Islamabad, Pakistan.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Basic and Applied Sciences, International Islamic University Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Basic and Applied Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A p-type transition metal oxide, copper oxide (CuO) was modified with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) to fabricate an effective CuO/NCQDs solar driven photo catalyst. The fabrication of single phase monoclinic CuO was confirmed by XRD. SEM images showed the leaf like morphology for CuO which appeared rough and densely packed in CuO/NCQDs composites. TEM images of CuO/NCQDs shows slightly distorted nano-leaves with NCQDs dispersed on them. HRTEM of CuO/NCQDs shows fringes with characteristic planes confirming that the addition of NCQDs has not distorted the crystal structure of CuO. SAED of CuO/NCQDs further confirms the crystalline nature of the as synthesized composite photo catalyst by exhibiting bright diffraction rings. These results further supported the XRD data. EDS spectra of NCQDs and CuO/NCQDs confirm the formation of pure nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and Cu, N, C and O atoms confirming that NCQDs are well incorporated on CuO Nano leaves. UV–Vis spectra showed a slight increase of band gap energies due to quantum confinement effects. PL spectra exhibited decreased photoluminescence intensity indicating suppression of recombination rate. The developed photocatalyst was applied for the degradation of harmful dye methyl orange. The composite catalyst showed superior degradation efficiency as compared to pure CuO nano-leaves attributed to enhanced visible light absorption and better charge separation ability due to introduction of NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The efficient utilization of the most abundant and sustainable solar light for energy and environmental applications can be a real and effective alternative to the rapidly decreasing fossil fuels. Despite of the fact that the solar energy is the most plentiful clean source of energy, still its utilization is very low. However, intense research has been triggered since last two decades to harvest the visible part of the solar spectrum by tuning various semiconducting nanomaterials for photocatalytic applications. The photoexcitation of these materials through solar light for decomposition of harmful toxic organic pollutants from industrial waste waters is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach for water purification as compared to the conventional methods. Transition metal oxides based nanomaterials have been investigated extensively by tuning their properties through doping, surface modification etc, for photocatalytic applications including dye degradation, environmental remediation and fuel generation and are considered to constitute the largest group of nanomaterials available for photocatalytic applications [1–6].

Copper oxide (CuO), a transition metal oxide with a narrow band gap of 1.2–1.4 eV is a promising p-type material with physiochemical properties strongly dependent upon its morphology and size. The CuO nanostructures has been reported in literature with different morphologies including hollow-spherical, nanoleaves, nano needles, nanoflakes, nanosheets, nanowires with multidimensional applications such as energy conversion and storage, air and water purification, sensors, electronics, and as an antibacterial materials [7–11]. The remarkable properties of CuO specially its photochemical stability, narrow band gap, non-toxicity and low synthesis cost enables its application as an efficient heterogeneous photocatalytic material under UV and/or visible light irradiation [12,13] Sanjini et al. have reported excellent efficiency of CuO nanoparticles sythesized through microwave assisted method starting with different precursors, towards photocatalytic degradation of MB and RhB [14]. Liu and co-worker attributed the photocatalytic efficiency improvement for methyl orange degradation using CuO/TiO2 catalyst towards Cu2+ and Cu+/Cu coexistence [15]. Cataño et al. have fabricated a Z-scheme based ZnO/CuO/Ag ternary nanocomposite and applied it for MO degradation proving an improved photocatalytic activity. They defined the role of CuO in the form of enhanced optical response of composite photocatalysts and supporting a Z-scheme based charge transfer mechanism [16]. Momeni et al. designed Ag–CuO and Pt–CuO nanostructures and investigated their photocatalytic activities for visible light induced photocatalytic degradation of MO [17,18].

The carbon derived nanomaterials are recently considered as an interesting topic of research for photocatalytic applications due to their facile low-cost synthesis, non-toxicity, photostability, biocompatibility and environmentally friendly nature. Among the most recently studied carbon nanomaterials, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has attracted the researchers greatly due to their unique properties, especially their up-converted photoluminescence and effective electron transfer ability enables them to be used either as a catalyst itself or in designing a photocatalyst [19]. Different research groups are trying to tune the properties of carbon-based quantum dots to use them as a photocatalyst or in composites with other materials. The doping of CQDs is considered as an effective strategy for tuning their properties for various applications. For example, by nitrogen doping, the synthesized NCQDs exhibit improved quantum yield and the doping with n-type material enhances the electron transfer process while on the other hand a p-type dopant promotes hole transfer process as reported by Barman et al. [20]. In recent years, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots have been applied for various photocatalytic reactions and have shown improved results. Shi et al. prepared CQDs with different nitrogen doping levels and analysed its effect on the performance of NCQDs/TiO2 catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and they reported that the composite catalyst exhibits better efficiency than pure TiO2. They also explained that the increased nitrogen doping level do not affect the stability of photo catalyst [21]. Chen et al. synthesized NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst and applied it for the degradation of bisphenol A and methyl orange and attributed the superior activity of the photocatalyst to the enhanced charge transfer properties of the NCQDs and to the surface plasmon resonance effect of silver nanoparticles [22].

In this study, we have fabricated an efficient photocatalyst by tuning the CuO nano-leaves with unique morphology with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (CuO/NCQDs) and investigated its photocatalytic activity towards dye degradation by taking methyl orange as a model dye under solar illumination. The role of NCQDs on the surface and optical properties of CuO nano leaves was discussed with detail. The increased roughness with the introduction of NCQDs raises the number of photoreaction sites and increases the photo adsorption. The photoluminescence quenching of composites as well as the quantum confinement effects on band gap of CuO nanomaterials and ultimately effect photodegradation efficiency. FTIR spectra depicts the corresponding functional groups of the pure and composite catalyst. The modification of optical properties of CuO nano-leaves is indicated by UV–Vis absorbance profile and PL spectra in the form of tuned bandgap energies and reduced charge carrier recombination rate, respectively. CuO/NCQDs composites have shown the improved photocatalytic activity as compared to the pure CuO and NCQDs. The stability of the composite catalyst was checked by repeating the degradation process for five cycles showing good stability. The addition of NCQDs has decreased the rate of charge carrier recombination which plays a major role in increasing photocatalytic activity

The required materials to experimentally synthesize the photocatalyst include copper nitrate Cu(NO3)2, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), citric acid and urea. All these materials were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and were of analytical grade. These were used without further purification.

The synthesis of CuO nanoleaves was accomplished through a sol-gel approach. Firstly, 4.0 g of copper nitrate was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and a blue colored solution was obtained. Then NaOH pellets were dissolved in distilled water until the pH 9–10 was obtained. Afterwards this NaOH solution was added dropwise to the coppernitrate solution and stirred followed by washings with distilled water and centrifuged to obtain nanostructures. The nanoparticles were dried at 60 ᵒC and finally calcined at 500 ᵒC for 2 h.

The nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized through a one step hydrothermal method. Typically 3.0g of each citric acid and urea was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water followed by a hydrothermal treatment at 180 ᵒC for 4h. After cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the obtained nitrogen doped CQDs solution was centrifuged at 4000rpm for 10 min to remove the larger particles.

The composite photocatalyst was fabricated by firstly dissolving 0.3 g of the synthesized CuO nanoleaves in distilled water and then by adding NCQDs in three different amounts 0.5 mL, 1.0 mL and 1.5 mL with magnetic stirring. The obtained CuO/NCQDs composites were dried in an oven at 60 ᵒC. The synthetic recipe of the samples is described in Scheme 1 .

The synthesized samples were characterized by using standard techniques. For the structural and phase analysis of the synthesized samples, a Rigaku D/Max 2550 VB/PC XRD machine with Cu Kα radiation having λ = 0.154056 nm was used. SEM images and EDX compositional mapping were performed by using JEOL-JAD-2300. TEM and HRTEM investigations were carried through a Tecnai F20 operated with 200 kV. Park NX10 AFM was used to perform the AFM analysis of NCQDs. UV–Vis spectrophotometer Cary 100 was used to study the UV absorbance of the synthesized samples. The photoluminescence spectra were obtained by using a PerkinElmer LS-50B luminescence spectrometer. The Bruker Alpha Eco-ATR was used for FTIR studies. Raman spectrometer with uRAMBOSS Dangoo Optron Co. Ltd. with excitation wavelength of 532.0 nm was employed to obtain Raman spectra.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized photocatalysts was tested by taking 20 ppm solution of methyl orange (MO) in distilled water as a model dye. 50 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of dye solution. The mixture was first stirred in dark for adsorption and then exposed to a solar simulator LCS-100 with an intensity of 100 m W/cm2 (one sun intensity) for 4 h. A thermostat was used to maintain the temperature of the reaction mixture to the room temperature. The samples were drawn after regular intervals of 1 h and centrifuged. UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to analyse the adsorption of the samples.

The remarkable properties of NCQDs were utilized for improving the solar-driven photocatalytic performance of CuO. The addition of NCQDs do not disturb the crystal structure of CuO nano-leaves rather surface roughness is found to increase making more active sites available. TEM and HRTEM images together with SAED patterns confirm the successful formation of CuO/NCQDs composite. FTIR spectra depicts the corresponding functional groups of the catalyst. The modification of optical properties of CuO nano-leaves is indicated by UV–Vis absorbance profile and PL spectra in the form of tuned bandgap energies and reduced charge carrier recombination rate, respectively.

In order to investigate the structural properties of NCQDs, XRD, FTIR and AFM was performed, and the obtained results are presented in Fig. 1 (A-C). Fig. 1A presents the XRD profile of NCQDs with a broad characteristic carbon peak at 2θ = 25ᵒ of (002) plane. The nitrogen doping has slightly shifted the peak. The FTIR spectrum (1B) of NCQDs shows a broad band between 3100 and 3400 cm−1 owing to O–H vibrations while CC and CN stretching vibrations are observed at 1593 cm−1 and 1438 cm−1 respectively [23]. Fig. 1C depicts the atomic force microscopy image for NCQDs. The NCQDs of the average size 5–6 nm are clearly seen with slight agglomeration. The same size of the NCQDs was later confirmed with HRTEM of composites.

X-ray diffractometer was used to identify the crystal structures, phase and to determine crystallite size. Fig. 2 (a–d) explains the XRD results of pure CuO (a) and CuO/NCQDs (b-d) with different concentrations of NCQDs. In case of pure CuO, XRD pattern exhibits the characteristics peaks indexed to 32.3ᵒ(110), 35.2ᵒ(
1 ‾
11
), 38.3ᵒ(111), 48.8ᵒ (
2
‾
02 )
, 53.2ᵒ(020), 57.9ᵒ(202), 61.3ᵒ(
1 ‾
13 )
, 66.0ᵒ(022), 67.9ᵒ(220), 75.2ᵒ(203) confirming the formation of single phase monoclinic CuO nanoparticles (JCPDS No. 045–0937) as no peaks of impurities or other phases are spotted [24,25]. The average particle size was calculated by Debye Scherer formula; D = 0.9 λ/β cosθ, where β is the FWHM and λ corresponds to the wavelength of X-ray. The average particle size is calculated to be 10.75 ± 0.32 nm. XRD patterns of various CuO/NCQDs composites are depicted in Fig. 2 with NCQDs 0.5 mL (b), 1.0 mL (c) and 1.5 mL (d). Addition of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots is confirmed by the appearance of a broad carbon peak around 30ᵒ which becomes more prominent with increasing concentration of NCQDs. Although, a considerable suppression of CuO peaks can be seen with NCQDs incorporation but no shifts in diffraction peaks of CuO was observed which indicates the stability of CuO phase structure [26]. Further, no additional peaks are observed in XRD pattern of CuO/NCQDs composites which shows that no chemical reaction has occurred between CuO and NCQDs [27].

The morphology of synthesized nanostructures was analysed by scanning electron microscopy. Fig. 3 (A-D) depicts the morphological pictures for pure CuO (A, B) and CuO/NCQDs composites with NCQD amount 1.5 mL (C, D) at different resolutions. SEM image of pure CuO suggests small randomly oriented floral sheet like structures. A very little agglomeration of the CuO Nano-sheets can be seen at some places which is attributed to their high surface tension and surface energy [28]. Some free spaces or voids can be clearly seen between floral sheets. At higher resolution, the CuO nanostructures appear to be thin leaf like nanosheets with the average thickness of sheets between 23.50 and 26.50 nm. The leaf like nanostructures are due to the low basicity conditions during the reaction [29]. SEM image of CuO/NCQDs shows that small transparent nanodots are distributed in CuO matrix. The addition of NCQDs has converted the leaf like CuO Nano-sheets into somewhat thicker, wrinkled, rough and dense packed Nano-leaves with a well coverage of voids thus enhancing the surface area for improved photocatalytic activity. The increased roughness with the introduction of NCQDs raises the number of photoreaction sites and increases the photo adsorption [30,31]. A slight distortion of Nano-leaves is also visible which is attributed to the decrease in surface tension among the matrix.

TEM and HRTEM images of pure CuO and CuO/NCQDs binary composite with NCQD amount 1.5 mL were taken at different resolutions and shown in Fig. 4 (A-E). The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns are also presented in Fig. 4 (C, F). TEM images of pure CuO (Fig. 4A) confirms the leaf like morphology consistent with the morphology shown by SEM. The HRTEM image of pure CuO (Fig. 4B) depicts fringes with d-spacing of 0.27 nm, 0.25 nm and 0.23 nm corresponding to the characteristic peaks from planes (110), (
1 ‾
11
), and (111) supporting the XRD results. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of CuO (Fig. 4C) further exhibits the bright ring pattern corresponding to characteristic d-spacing values of monoclinic CuO nanostructures. It confirms the single crystalline phase of the CuO Nano leaves. TEM images of CuO/NCQDs (Fig. 4D) shows slightly distorted Nano leaves with NCQDs dispersed on them. HRTEM of composite photocatalyst (Fig. 4E) shows fringes with the characteristic planes confirming that the addition of NCQDs has not distorted the crystal structure of CuO. The size of the NCQDs from HRTEM is found to be 5.0 nm. SAED of CuO/NCQDs (Fig. 4F) further confirms crystalline nature of as synthesized composite photo catalyst by exhibiting bright diffraction rings [32–35].

The elemental mapping data of the synthesized samples was obtained using energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) which is shown in Fig. 5 (A-C). The EDS spectrum of CuO (Fig. 5A) confirms the synthesis of pure CuO showing the peaks of Cu and O only with no other impurities. The existence of N, C and O peaks in the EDS spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. 5B) confirms the formation of pure nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots. The spectrum of CuO/NCQDs (Fig. 5C) consists of Cu, N, C and O atoms confirming that NCQDs are well incorporated on CuO nano-leaves. Table 1 presents the relative compositions of synthesized samples.

The optical absorption ability and bandgap calculations of synthesized photocatalysts were performed by analysing the UV–Vis spectra shown in Fig. 6 (A-C). UV–Vis curve of CuO nanoparticles (Fig. 6Aa) shows weak absorption with small fundamental absorption edge around 300 nm. The spectra of NCQDs (Fig. 6Ab) show absorption peak at 230 nm and shoulder peak around 350 nm corresponding to π-π* transitions of CC bond and n-π* transitions of CN and CO bonds respectively [36]. The absorption tail extends to visible region at around 600 nm. CuO/NCQDs composite with NCQDs amount of 0.5 mL (Fig. 6Ac) shows well enhanced light absorption with tail extending to 800 nm exhibiting a full coverage of visible region and also the NCQDs peak around 350 nm is slightly shifted to longer wavelength. By increasing the NCQDs concentration to 1.5 mL, the absorption peaks still higher but the absorption edge is blue shifted and the shoulder peak of NCQDs disappears (Fig. 6Ad).

The band gaps of synthesized nanomaterials were determined by drawing the corresponding Tauc plots as shown in Fig. 6B for direct and Fig. 6C for indirect bandgap. The difference between the direct and indirect bandgap energies defines the crystalline behaviour of nanomaterials. The higher values of direct bandgap energies in comparison to indirect bandgap energies show that the material is crystalline [37,38]. The indirect band gap energy of the synthesized pure CuO nanoparticles is 1.8 eV which is higher than the reported bulk value (1.2 eV). The enhanced band gap energy is due to smaller size of the synthesized nanoparticles [39]. The indirect bandgap energies of CuO/NCQDs composites are determined to be 2.0 eV which falls in the required range of bandgap for producing hydroxyl radicals for pollutant degradation. This slight increase of indirect bandgap energies of the composites than the pure CuO is attributed to the quantum confinement effects [40,41]. The direct band gap energies of pure CuO and the composites are found to be 3.1 and 3.3eV, respectively showing a blue shift of the bandgap values in composites [42]. These values are higher than the indirect bandgap energies. Hence, the synthesized nano-composites are crystalline in nature which are in consistent with the XRD results.

The photoluminescent spectra of pure CuO and CuO/NCQDs binary composites with NCQDs amounts of 0.5 mL and 1.0 mL at an excitation wavelength of 270 nm are shown in Fig. 7 . Pure CuO nanocrystals (Fig. 7a) show multiple PL emission bands at 348 nm, 558 nm and 650 nm and two shoulder peaks at 390 nm and 461 nm. These results are consistent with the results reported in literature [43,44]. The strong emission peak at 348 nm comes from band edge emissions resulted from the electron-hole recombination of CuO free excitons. The peak at 461 nm can be ascribed to the surface impurities, e.g. oxygen vacancies, intrinsic defects, interstitial metal ions in oxide, of the p-type semiconductor CuO. On the other hand, the deep level defects give rise to an emission peak at 558 nm. The peak at 650 nm is ascribed to the singly ionized oxygen vacancies or to the multiple oxidation states of copper [45–47]. PL spectra of composites (Fig. 7 b, c) indicate a noticeable reduction of the photoluminescence intensity. It is observed that the peaks at 390, 461 and 650 nm totally disappear while the others at 348 and 558 nm show significantly minimized photoluminescence intensity. The PL signals infact originate from photo induced charge-carrier recombination. Hence a lower PL intensity corresponds to reduced recombination rate. In this way, the obtained results confirm that the addition of NCQDs has decreased the rate of charge carrier recombination which plays a major role in increasing photocatalytic activity [48].

The FTIR spectra of synthesized CuO nanostructures and CuO/NCQDs composites are shown in Fig. 8 (a–d) which explain the presence of different functional groups in the synthesized nanomaterials. For pure CuO, the characteristic FTIR signals are usually observed in the range 400–650 cm−1. However, sometimes an additional peak around 1383 cm−1 is reported by different groups which corresponds to Cu (II) O stretching vibrations [49]. In our study, the synthesized CuO nano-leaves (Fig. 8a) have shown characteristic IR peaks at 384 cm−1, 487 cm−1 and 597 cm−1. All these peaks are attributed to the Cu–O stretching vibrations [50–52]. No additional peak for Cu(II) O stretching vibrations is observed which confirms that the formed nanostructures only comprises of pure CuO and no mixed phase is present. This is also proved by the XRD results. FTIR spectra of the composites in Fig. 8(b–d) show a relatively broad band between 1440 and 1650 cm−1 which is due to the CC and C–N stretching vibrations [53–55]. Furthermore, the addition of NCQDs has enhanced the FTIR signals of CuO with a shift of the FTIR bands of Cu–O. This shift is found to be more pronounced with NCQDs amounts of 1.0 and 1.5 mL.

The Raman spectrum of CuO/NCQDs composite with 1.5mL NCQDs is shown in Fig. 9 . The characteristic Raman peaks for CuO are observed at 266 cm−1 and 600 cm−1. The CuO is reported to have nine optical phonon modes having symmetries Ag + 2Bg + 4Au + 5Bu. Out of these nine optical modes, only three (Ag + 2Bg) are Raman-active. The peak at 266 cm−1 is assigned to Ag while the peak at 600 cm−1 is assigned to Bg modes of vibration. These values of wavenumbers are slightly lower than the values reported in literature because of the grain size effects. With increasing grain size, the peak shifts to higher wavenumbers [56,57]. The characteristic signal for carbon is not detected. This is due to the fact that the NCQDs are added in very small amounts.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanoparticles was investigated by solar induced photodegradation of organic dye, MO in distilled water at room temperature. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 10 (A) . The straight-line curve shows the degradation of MO in the absence of the catalyst, suggesting no degradation of MO. The pure CuO nano-leaves has shown 33.0% degradation after 4 h illumination without using any hole scavenger. The low solar induced degradation efficiency can be due to the fast recombination rate owing to very small bandgap energy.

The CuO/NCQDs composites have shown a degradation of 63.0%, 81.0%, 92.0% and 86% with varying amounts of NCQDs i.e. 0.5 mL (c), 1.0 mL (d), 1.5 mL (f) 2.0 mL (e) respectively in Fig. 10A. This clearly depicts that the incorporation of NCQDs to CuO has increased the degradation efficiency of CuO. This is in fact due to the electron trapping ability of the NCQDs which reduces the rate of recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The NCQDs further extends the visible light absorption capacity which is also desirable for improving photocatalytic activity of nanomaterials. However, by increasing the amount of N-CQDs to 2 ml the photocatalytic performance was decreased. This is because the opacity of NCQDs results in light scattering which decreases the photocatalytic performance. Hence the best photocatalytic activity was observed with NCQDs amount of 1.5 mL.

The recycle ability of the composite CuO/NCQDs photocatalyst (Fig. 11 ) was investigated by repeating the photocatalytic experiments for five cycles. The obtained results showed only a slight decrease (10%) of the degradation efficiency which can be attributed to the loss of the catalyst amount during the filtration process. Hence the synthesized catalyst is proved to be highly stable and can be applied for photodegradation purposes efficiently.

Fig. 12 shows the XRD and SEM analysis of the CuO/NCQDs nanocomposites. XRD profile clearly shows that no change in peaks has occurred after photocatalytic experiments. The inset depicts that the morphology of the nano photocatalyst is also retained confirming the stability of the photocatalyst .

The mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of MO through CuO/NCQDs photocatalyst is proposed in Scheme 2 . Basically, the photocatalytic reaction requires the conduction band of the photocatalyst to be more negative and the valence band to be more positive than the corresponding potentials of the redox couples present in the solution. This enables the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs to proceed oxidation and reduction reactions. The bulk CuO has a very small bandgap energy (1.2 eV) insufficient to carry redox reactions. However, the synthesized Nano-scale CuO exhibits the indirect bandgap energy of 1.8 eV because the quantum confinement effects come into play due to smaller size and then coupling with NCQDs further increased the bandgap to 2.0 eV [58–60] The process starts by the generation of the electron-hole pairs when the surface of the photocatalyst is irradiated with solar light. The electrons move to the conduction band. The nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots act as electron reservoirs and they trap the electrons and suppress the recombination process hence making more charge carriers available for redox reactions. These electrons then convert molecular oxygen to superoxide anion radical O2 .-, which reacts with water molecules to produce hydrogen peroxide and hydroxyl radicals. The holes on the other end directly react with the hydroxyl radical and convert them to .OH radicals which then oxidizes the dye into harmless products. In this way, the suppression of recombination rate through quantum confinement effects assures the increased solar induced photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CuO/NCQDs catalyst.

In this research work, a highly efficient visible light driven photocatalyst was developed by incorporating robust and easy to synthesize nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with varying amountsinto CuO nano-leaves. The developed photocatalyst was applied for water detoxification. The bulk CuO having very narrow band gap suffers from rapid charge carrier recombination rate. It was observed that the quantum confinement effects of NCQDs tuned the bandgap of the CuO nanostructures to the required values for efficient photocatalysis. The photoluminescence of the composite was greatly quenched which indicated the reduced recombination rate. The electron trapping ability of the NCQDs which reduces the rate of recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Also, the surface roughness indicated the availability of more active sites for carrying redox reactions. The highest photocatalytic activity was shown by the composites with NCQDs amount of 1.5 mL. The synthesized photo catalyst was found fairly stable, cost effective with facile synthetic approach. Therefore, it is suggested that it can be applied for the solar light driven water detoxification efficiently.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by International Islamic University Pakistan (IIUI), Pakistan Institute of Engineering and applied Sciences (PIEAS) and Higher Education Commission (NRPU Grant 3660). We are also thankful to Government College University, Allama Iqbal Open University, National Institute of Laser and Optronics, University of the Punjab and Government College University (GCU) Lahore for analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2019.109233.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-metal doping of transition metal oxides for visible-light photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Marschall, R., & Wang, L. (2014). Non-metal doping of transition metal oxides for visible-light photocatalysis. Catal. Today, 225, 111-135.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper impregnated ionic liquid assisted mesoporous titania: visible light photo catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sajjad, S., Leghari, S. A. K., & Zhang, J. (2013). Copper impregnated ionic liquid assisted mesoporous titania: visible light photo catalyst. RSC Adv., 3(31), 12678-12687.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen, carbon and iron multiple-co doped titanium dioxide nanotubes as a new high-performance photo catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Ahadzadeh, I., & Rahmati, A. (2016). Nitrogen, carbon and iron multiple-co doped titanium dioxide nanotubes as a new high-performance photo catalyst. Journal of Materials Science: Materials in Electronics, 27(8), 8646-8653.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication, characterization and photoelectrochemical properties of cuprous oxide-reduced graphene oxide photocatalysts for hydrogen generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Ghayeb, Y., & Menati, M. (2018). Fabrication, characterization and photoelectrochemical properties of cuprous oxide-reduced graphene oxide photocatalysts for hydrogen generation. Journal of Materials Science: Materials in Electronics, 29(5), 4136-4146.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication, characterization and photoelectrochemical activity of tungsten-copper co-sensitized TiO2 nanotube composite photoanodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Ghayeb, Y., & Ezati, F. (2018). Fabrication, characterization and photoelectrochemical activity of tungsten-copper co-sensitized TiO2 nanotube composite photoanodes. Journal of colloid and interface science, 514, 70-82.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and characterization of copper doped TiO2 nanotube arrays by in situ electrochemical method as efficient visible-light photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Ghayeb, Y., & Ghonchegi, Z. (2015). Fabrication and characterization of copper doped TiO2 nanotube arrays by in situ electrochemical method as efficient visible-light photocatalyst. Ceramics International, 41(7), 8735-8741.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tadjarodi, A., Akhavan, O., & Bijanzad, K. (2015). Photocatalytic activity of CuO nanoparticles incorporated in mesoporous structure prepared from bis (2-aminonicotinato) copper (II) microflakes. Transactions of Nonferrous Metals Society of China, 25(11), 3634-3642.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hazarika, M., Das, J., Bordoloi, M., Borah, D. J., Das M. R., (2014) Bio-derived CuO nanoparticles for the photocatalytic treatment of dyes Chandan Tamuly a,n , Mater. Letters 123 202-205

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlled synthesis of highly ordered CuO nanowire arrays by template-based sol-gel route

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Su, Y. K., Shen, C. M., Yang, H. T., Li, H. L., & Gao, H. J. (2007). Controlled synthesis of highly ordered CuO nanowire arrays by template-based sol-gel route. Transactions of Nonferrous Metals Society of China, 17(4), 783-786.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano-structured CuO films prepared by simple solution methods: plate-like, needle-like and network-like architectures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bayansal, F., Cetinkara, H. A., Kahraman, S., Cakmak, H. M., & Guder, H. S. (2012). Nano-structured CuO films prepared by simple solution methods: plate-like, needle-like and network-like architectures. Ceram. International, 38(3), 1859-1866.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and green synthesis of CuO nanoneedles with high photo catalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Ghayeb, Y., & Menati, M. (2016). Facile and green synthesis of CuO nanoneedles with high photo catalytic activity. Journal of Materials Science: Materials in Electronics, 27(9), 9454-9460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of copper oxide nanoparticle and photocatalytic dye degradation study using response surface methodology (RSM) and genetic algorithm (GA)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mahmoodi, N. M., Keshavarzi, S., & Rezaei, P. (2017). Synthesis of copper oxide nanoparticle and photocatalytic dye degradation study using response surface methodology (RSM) and genetic algorithm (GA). Desalination and Water Treatment, 72, 394-405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of copper decorated tungsten oxide–titanium oxide nanotubes by photochemical deposition technique and their photocatalytic application under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M. (2015). Fabrication of copper decorated tungsten oxide-titanium oxide nanotubes by photochemical deposition technique and their photocatalytic application under visible light. Applied Surface Science, 357, 160-166.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sanjini, N. S., Winston, B., & Velmathi, S. (2017). Effect of precursors on the synthesis of CuO nanoparticles under microwave for photocatalytic activity towards methylene blue and Rhodamine B dyes. J. Nanosci. Nanotechnology, 17(1), 495-501.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, Z., & Zhou, C. (2015). Improved photocatalytic activity of nano CuO-incorporated TiO2 granules prepared by spray drying. Progress in Natural Science: Materials International, 25(4), 334-341.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Catano, F. A., Caceres, G., Burgos, A., & Schrebler, R. S. (2018). Synthesis and characterization of a ZnO/CuO/Ag composite and its application as a photocatalyst for methyl orange degradation. Int. J. Electrochem. Sci, 13, 9242-9256.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Momeni, M. M., Mirhosseini, M., & Mohammadi, N. (2016). Efficient photo catalytic degradation of methyl orange over Ag-CuO nanostructures grown on copper foil under visible light irradiation. Journal of Materials Science: Materials in Electronics, 27(6), 6542-6551.
